findings.title: Python bytes assignment does not zero memory
findings.description: Setting flow.request.content = b"" and flow.response.content = b"" replaces the object reference but does not zero the underlying bytes in memory. The original bytes object (potentially containing POST body with credentials, tokens, or other sensitive data) remains in the Python heap until the GC collects it. In Python there is no native way to zero bytes objects, so this is an inherent language limitation rather than a code defect.
findings.impact: Sensitive request/response body data (credentials in POST bodies, auth tokens) may remain in process memory for an indeterminate period after the reference is dropped. A memory dump of the process could expose this data longer than intended.
findings.recommendation: Accepted limitation for Python. The current approach (dropping the reference) is the standard Python pattern and still reduces average memory retention time. No code change required. Document this as a known limitation if CA key material or credentials frequently appear in captured bodies.

notes: No hardcoded secrets, API keys, or credentials found.
No injection vectors — FlowCleanup only assigns b"" or clears lists, no user input processed.
Access control not applicable — addon runs within mitmproxy's internal hook chain, not exposed externally.
WebSocket message trimming (keep last 1) reduces retained data exposure surface — positive security effect.
Addon ordering (FlowCleanup registered last after GeoFixAddon) is correct: GeoFixAddon processes flows first, cleanup runs after. No race condition possible in mitmproxy's sequential hook dispatch.
No SSRF risk — no URL construction or external HTTP calls.
Test file diff was not included in the provided diff, so test code was not audited.
